coredata.dc:title: Efficacy of anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities of carbon quantum dots synthesized from sugarcane bagasse and pith
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

John P Paul Winston, Albin
Mohamed, K.M.
Sagayaraj, P.
Madhavan, J.
Rajesh Kumar, S.
Amala Jayanthi, S.
Judith Vijaya, J.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) produced from sugarcane bagasse (SB) and sugarcane bagasse pith (SBP) were synthesized, by hydrothermal method. Analytical methods such as, UV–Visible spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Photoluminescence (PL), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), were used to characterize the synthesized CQDs (SB and SBP). Furthermore, the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of CQDs SB and SBP were assessed. The synthesized CQDs SB and SBP have spherical morphology, as shown by HRTEM investigation, and with a particle size of about 5 nm. Using the brine shrimp lethality assay, the toxicity of CQDs SB and SBP were investigated at various doses. Through their ability to scavenge H2O2 radicals, CQDs SB and SBP were studied with strong anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. This work sheds light on CQDs, which demonstrated powerful antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sugarcane bagasse
Pith
Carbon Quantum Dots
Cytotoxicity
Antioxidant
Anti-inflammatory

FTIR spectra of (a) SB and (b) SBP.
HRTEM micrographs at different magnifications for (a − d) SB and (e − h) SBP.
PL Emission spectra of (a) SB and (b) SBP.
Raman spectra of SB and SBP.

Zeta potential of (a) SB and (b) SBP.
Photo stability of SB and SBP.
Cytotoxic effect of live nauplii for (a) SB and (b) SBP.
Anti-inflammatory activity of SB and SBP.
Anti-oxidant activity of SB and SBP.
Comparison of quantum yield using different precursors of CQDs.

head.ce:title: Efficacy of anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities of carbon quantum dots synthesized from sugarcane bagasse and pith

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) produced from sugarcane bagasse (SB) and sugarcane bagasse pith (SBP) were synthesized, by hydrothermal method. Analytical methods such as, UV–Visible spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Photoluminescence (PL), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), were used to characterize the synthesized CQDs (SB and SBP). Furthermore, the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of CQDs SB and SBP were assessed. The synthesized CQDs SB and SBP have spherical morphology, as shown by HRTEM investigation, and with a particle size of about 5 nm. Using the brine shrimp lethality assay, the toxicity of CQDs SB and SBP were investigated at various doses. Through their ability to scavenge H2O2 radicals, CQDs SB and SBP were studied with strong anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. This work sheds light on CQDs, which demonstrated powerful antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent trends and innovations in science and technology have led to the discovery of various allotropes of carbon. These allotropes have proven to be suitable for a wide range of direct or multiple applications [1]. Since 2006, Sun and his team members pioneered the art of synthesising fluorescent carbon nanoparticles, which were later labelled as Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) possessing unique polychromatic property across the visible spectrum [2]. As a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials, CQDs have garnered significant interest in dealing with optical, biomedical, catalysis, solar cells supercapacitors, batteries etc. Generally speaking, carbon quantum dots refer to monodispersed fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials with stable photoluminescence characteristics and are smaller than 10 nm in size with roughly spherical configuration [3–5]. The majority of CQDs have carboxyl groups on their surface, which provides them with better solubility in water and enabled surface passivation and further functionalization with related organic polymers. Many studies have been carried out in recent years depicting CQDs with an inherent characteristic such as optical property [6], water solubility, excellent thermal conductivity [7], biocompatibility, and tunable photoluminescence [8]. Based on these qualities, CQDs has a wide range of applications in biomedical fields [9], including drug delivery [10], gene transfection [11], biomarkers [12], bioimaging[13], and biosensing [14].

The current trends in nanotechnology deals with the conversion of biomass waste into innovative nanomaterials for a range of applications as a promising example for “waste to wealth”[15]. The excessive disposal of household and agricultural products has led to the accumulation of biodegradable waste, which is the primary cause of pollution in land and water ecosystems. Regarding this aspect of contamination, a straightforward method for adding value to various biomass wastes, by turning them into carbon nanoparticles. However, a variety of methods have been carried out by researchers focusing on the production of carbon nanomaterials from various biomass wastes.

One of the main biomass wastes produced annually worldwide is sugarcane bagasse (SCB). Sugarcane bagasse is the most available form of biodegradable waste in comparison with any market scaled products. Hence, we have focussed on the preparation of CQDs from bagasse than other raw material. Moreover, the end products from sugar industry are being used as fuel in boilers and is supplied to the paper industry. In order to generate alternative byproducts from bagasse, we have opted to synthesize CQD, which possesses a higher economic value in comparison to paper. However, in countries like India, Brazil, and China, the majority of sugarcane bagasse is discarded as biomass waste. India producing about 350 MT. of sugarcane annually, making it the world's second-largest producer behind Brazil [16]. Every year, sugarcane production yields between 40 and 50 % bagasse, or dry pulp and fibrous residues. This produces serious pollution to the land and water, necessitating the efficient handling of these solid wastes. For their sustainable use, a new strategy for managing such industrial biomass wastes is thus desperately needed.

Resource degradation and environmental problems are likely outcomes of this waste. One intriguing approach to high-quality bagasse utilization is the conversion of discarded sugarcane bagasse into new types of nanomaterials. Numerous lignocellulosic components, including cellulose (38 %), hemicellulose (28 %), and lignin (18 %), are present in sugarcane bagasse (SB). [17,18]. Similarly, Sugarcane bagasse pith (SBP) a by-product obtained in sugarcane processing factories, comprises of approximately 50 % cellulose, 25 % hemicellulose, and 25 % lignin. Because of these components, sugarcane bagasse is a great source of carbon for carbon-based products such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene, biochar, and nanocrystals [19,20].

However, in the present report, we have investigated CQDs' nonlinear optical characteristics using industrial agricultural waste (sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith) as a carbon source synthesized by hydrothermal method. The synthesized CQDs were evaluated for its toxicity, antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects.

Sugarcane bagasse (SB) and sugarcane bagasse pith (SBP) were collected from Kothari Sugars and Chemicals Ltd, Sathamangalam village, Vettriyur Post Ariyalur District – 621707, Tamil Nadu and citric acid was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots of SB and SBP
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained sugarcane bagasse and sugarcane bagasse pith were powdered and washed thoroughly with double distilled water, boiled, filtered, and dried. 2 g of the sugarcane bagasse (SB) and 1 g of Polyethylene glycol were mixed with 70 ml of milli – Q − water. Then, the mixture was hydrothermally treated at 180 °C for 2 h, the solution was filtered with Whatman filter paper without further addition of water and dialysed for 2 days with a dialysis bag and the obtained filtrate is labelled as CQD (SB). It is used for further characterization and analysis, and a similar route is followed to prepare CQD (SBP).

The synthesized solutions were analysed by different methods, UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy analysis was carried out using U-2900 HITACHI spectrophotometer. Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) analysis was performed on an IR TRACER − 100 SHIMADZU spectrometer. Additionally, Using JEOL Japan's JEM-2100 Plus, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) examination was used to obtain important insights into the morphology and microstructural properties of the carbon quantum dots. The specific photoluminescence (PL) setup used in the study was studied in a Flurolog HORIBA. A certain light wavelength was used to excite the carbon quantum dots, and the resulting fluorescence was measured to record the PL spectra. Raman spectroscopy was carried out using WiTec alpha 300, Germany. Similarly, Zeta potential was performed on a Malvern/Nano ZS-90.

Using quinine sulphate as a reference, whose quantum yield is known (0.54), the integrated photoluminescence intensities and the sample absorbance values were compared in order to determine the quantum yield (Φ) of the synthesised CQDs SB and SBP. The following equation was used to compute the quantum yield equ (1).
(1)
∅ =
∅ R
[ I
A R
n
2
]
/
[
I R
n
R
2
]
where I and IR denote the integrated photoluminescence intensities of the sample and the reference; A and AR denote the absorbance values of the sample and the reference; n and nR denote the refractive indices of the sample and reference, respectively; and Φ and ΦR represent the quantum yield values of the sample and the reference [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxic studies by brine shrimp lethality assay

ce:section.ce:para: 200 ml of distilled water was used and to this 2 g of iodine-free salt was dissolved. 10– 12 mlof saline water were added to 6 ELISA well plates, and ten nauplii were gently added to each well (20 μL, 40 μL, 60 μL, 80 μL, 100 μL). The CQD from SB and SBP were then added following the concentration. For 24 h, the plates were incubated and the ELISA plates were inspected to determine the quantity of live nauplii present [22].

This was then computed using the formula.
(2)
%
o f
l i v e
n a u p l i i
=
n u m b e r
o f
d e a d
n a u p l i i
n u m b e r
o f
d e a d
n a u p l i i +
+
n u m b e r
o f
l i v e
n a u p l i i
×
100

For the egg albumin denaturation experiment, 2.8 mL of phosphate buffer and 0.2 mL of fresh egg albumin were utilised. In addition, the reaction mixture was enhanced with CQDs SB and SBP at varying doses (10–50 µg/mL). And the pH of the solution was maintained at 6.3. After being left at room temperature for ten minutes, it was incubated for thirty minutes at 55 °C in a water bath. Dimethyl sulphoxide served as the control group, and diclofenac sodium served as the standard group. After that, spectrophotometric measurements were performed on the samples at 660 nm. [23].

The percentage of denaturation in proteins were computed using the formula:
(3)
%
i n h i b i t i o n
=
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
-
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
s a m p l e
A b s o r b a n c e
o f
c o n t r o l
×
100

The approach suggested by Halliwell et al. was utilised to assess the antioxidant activity using the hydroxyl radical scavenging assay. To put it succinctly, 100 µL of 28 mM 2-deoxy-2-ribose was combined to create 1 mL of reaction mixture. CQDs SB and SBP were introduced at different concentrations (10–50 µg/mL) to that. 100 µL of ascorbic acid, 200 µL of EDTA, and 200 µL of 200 µM ferric chloride were also added. Following a one-hour incubation period at 37 °C, the optical density was assessed at 532 nm in comparison to the blank solution, with vitamin E serving as a positive control. The following equation was used to evaluated the percentage inhibition of H2O2 radical formation [24].
(4)
H y d r o x y l
r a d i c a l
s c a v e n g i n g
a c t i v i t y
( % )
=
A
b l a n k
-
A
s a m p l e
A
b l a n k
×
100
where, A sample is the absorbance of the reaction containing the sample; A blank is the absorbance of the control reaction (without sample).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para:
To comprehend the optical properties, we employed UV–Visible, PL, and FTIR spectroscopy. These methods were instrumental in elucidating and improving the overall clarity and coherence of the information. The bandgap associated with the synthesized material has been calculated by converting the UV absorbance plot to a Tauc plot resulting in the obtained bandgap. From the UV– Visible spectra Fig. 1 . (a), the excitation for the synthesized CQD SB was observed at 274 nm
π →
π
∗
transition and CQD SBP was seen at 279 nm corresponding to
π →
π
∗
transition. By employing the energy equation, we were able to calculate the bandgap to be 4.5 eV for CQD SB Fig. 1. (b) and 4.4 eV for CQD SBP Fig. 1. (c). The obtained bandgap is in concurrence with the previously reported articles [25,26].

The FTIR spectra of CQDs SB and SBP illustrated in Fig. 2 . (a) and (b). It is clear that the characteristic absorption peak at 3246 cm−1 corresponds to the H-bonded OH, and the O–H systems of carboxylic acid present axial stretching and shows a wide band in the region of 3315 cm−1, 1629 cm−1 corresponds to –C=O / COOH stretching vibrations and the band between 2109 and 2200 cm−1, which is associated with the alkyne function's secondary feature C≡C stretching band [27,28].

The HRTEM micrographs illustrated in Fig. 3 . (a,b,e and f) show the spherical bodies relating to CQDs detailing the structure. The size distributions of the extracted CQDs SB and SBP are illustrated in the histogram Fig. 3d and 3 h). The average diameter of the CQD was found to be around between 2 – 5 nm for both SB and SBP. The crystalline nature of CQDs was studied by visualizing the SAED pattern (Fig. 3e and 3 g) and comparable to the reported work [29].

Between 320 and 400 nm, the excitation wavelength significantly impacts the emission wavelength of CQDs SB and SBP. The emitted fluorescence intensity for CQD SB was observed to increase from 320 to 380 nm, followed by a gradual increase from 380 to 400 nm, as shown in Fig. 4 . (a). At 380 nm, the fluorescence intensity peaked indicating the emission wavelength related to a red shift, and the CQDs' excitation-dependent PL behaviour demonstrated the impact of the particle sizes. Likewise, for CQD SBP the intensity of the fluorescence released rose from 320 to 360 nm and subsequently fell from 360 to 400 nm, as shown in Fig. 4. (b). When stimulated at 360 nm, the fluorescence intensity peaked, the emission wavelength exhibited a red shift, and the CQDs' excitation-dependent photoluminescence behaviour demonstrated the impact of the particle sizes [30,31].

The recorded Raman spectra for both SB and SBP are displayed in Fig. 5 . SB exhibits two distinct Raman bands, one is a sharp and an intense band observed at 1587 cm−1, known as the G-band. The other weaker band observed at 1359 cm−1, referred to as the D-band. The D-band arises from the breathing mode of the A1g phonon and sp2 hybridized carbon domains. On the other hand, the G-band results from the scattering of the E2g phonon of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. Similarly, in SBP, a D-band at 1351 cm−1 is present, indicating the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon systems and a G-band at 1599 cm−1 confirming the elongation of bonds. The G-band is extremely sensitive to strain effects in the sp2 system. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) for SB and SBP was calculated and found to be 0.98 for both the CQD’s [32].

The Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum analysis shown in Fig. 6 , clearly demonstrates the presence of carbon along with oxygen. The SB sample (Fig. 6 a) contains 95.59 wt% carbon, while the SBP sample (Fig. 6 b) contains 90.46 wt%. Proving that the synthesis process employed is successful in generating CQDs with a significant level of carbon purity.

The zeta potential of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) determine the stability in suspension, surface charge, and interactions with other materials and biological systems. SB and SBP possess a potential of −5.98 mV and −3.78 mV respectively. It indicates that the CQDs SB and SBP have a negative surface charge as seen in Fig. 7 (a) and (b) respectively. This negative charge arises from the presence of functional groups on the surface of the CQDs that ionizes in the solution, resulting in an accumulation of negatively charged particles around the CQDs. The presence of surface functional groups like Carboxyl (–COOH), Hydroxyl (–OH) etc. on CQDs is responsible for their negative charge and the migration towards the positive electrode in an electric field, resulting in negative values for zeta potential. It demonstrates a better stability, that can remain well-dispersed in the solvent without settling or coagulating.

Herein, the as-prepared CQDs both SB and SBP (Fig. 8 ) were exposed under a 300 W (W1D2) lamp for various time spans to probe their photo-stability. The intensity of as-prepared CQDs decreased only slightly, and preserved ∼ 85 % for SB and ∼ 89 % of the initial intensity even after 120 min irradiation [33].

The study compares the quantum yield of various CQDs precursor synthesized through different methods and their applications (Table 1 ). Pomelo peels were used as a precursor, undergoing hydrothermal treatment at 220 °C. This process resulted in carbon dots with a quantum yield of 6.9 %, suitable for Hg2+ sensing applications (Wenbo et al., 2012)​. Candle soot, oxidized using HNO3, produced carbon dots with a quantum yield of 3 %, demonstrating potential for bioimaging applications (Ray et al., 2009)​​. Similarly, strawberry juice underwent hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C, yielding carbon dots with a quantum yield of 6.3 %, also effective for Hg2+ sensing (Hong et al., 2013). Ascorbic acid, subjected to heat treatment at 90 °C, resulted in carbon dots with a quantum yield of 3.22 %, suitable for pH sensing (Xiaofang et al., 2012). Sugar cane juice treated hydrothermally at 120 °C produced carbon dots with a quantum yield of 5.76 %, applicable in bioimaging (Vaibhavkumar et al., 2014)​​.

When ascorbic acid was treated hydrothermally at 180 °C, it produced carbon dots with a quantum yield of 6 % (Bing et al., 2010)​. Grass, treated hydrothermally at temperatures ranging from 150 °C to 200 °C, yielded carbon dots with quantum yields between 2.5 % and 6.2 %, effective for Cu2+ sensing (Sen et al., 2012)​. Lastly, sugarcane bagasse (SB) and sugarcane bagasse pith (SBP), treated hydrothermally at 180 °C, produced carbon dots with quantum yields of 6.0 % and 6.4 %, respectively. These were shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties in the present work​.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicological evaluation of CQDs SB and SBP
ce:section.ce:para: The brine shrimp lethality assay, which is a crucial screening technique to assess a drug's toxicity, is depicted in Fig. 9 . The percentage of living nauplii in the cytotoxic effect was computed. Day 1 results indicated 100 % live nauplii at various concentrations of synthesized CQDs SB and SBP as well as in the control. In SB, Except for 80 μg/mL, which demonstrated 90 % live nauplii, a mild reduction is due to the diverse concentration of CQDs [41]. In comparison, all the other concentrations showed 100 % survival as compared to the untreated control (Fig. 9a). Similarly, SBP demonstrated 100 % live nauplii on the second day at all concentrations (Fig. 9b). Based on the findings, it was concluded that the synthetic CQDs were safe for brine shrimps up to 40 μg/mL [42].

The study is to examine how certain materials exhibit anti inflammatory property by the denaturation of egg albumin assay, as it can potentially modify the physical and functional characteristics of the protein. It is essential to prevent the denaturation of egg albumin in order to reduce tissue damage and initiate inflammation. Therefore, materials that can decrease the denaturation of egg albumin may be considered as potential alternatives for anti-inflammatory activity. The immune system's intricate reaction to infection and damage, is known as inflammation, and it results in the elimination of harmful substances as well as the repair of tissue integrity and physiological processes. The two main categories arising are acute and chronic, depending on how long the inflammatory responses last. Loss of control over this intricate mechanism can result in the emergence of several inflammatory illnesses, even though it was first observed as a protective phenomenon [43]. The material's capacity to prevent proteins from denaturing indicates that it may have anti-inflammatory properties. Through a variety of non-covalent interactions, such as electrostatic, van der Waals, and hydrogen bonding, CQDs can interact with the albumin protein, preventing denaturation and stabilizing its structure. Significant inhibition was observed in a dose-dependent manner at 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg/mL, according to the data. Based on the study, in SB 50 % of inhibition was observed at a concentration 10 μg/mL, 59 % of inhibition at 20 μg/mL, 63 % of inhibition at 30 μg/mL, 68 % of inhibition at 40 μg/mL and maximum anti-inflammatory activity with 79 % of inhibition at 50 μg/mL (Fig. 10 ). Similarly, in SBP 50 % of inhibition was observed at a concentration 10 μg/mL, 62 % of inhibition at 20 μg/mL, 63 % of inhibition at 30 μg/mL, 70 % of inhibition at 40 μg/mL and maximum anti-inflammatory activity with 77 % of inhibition at 50 μg/mL. CQDs SB and SBP shows marked anti-inflammatory activity. The results revealed that the SB and SBP were potent which was comparable with that of standard diclofenac sodium.

The antioxidant activity plays a crucial role in assessing the ability of the synthesized materials to prevent cell damage. The degree to which CQDs mitigate this damage is investigated through antioxidant analysis. In the scavenging experiment, H2O2 molecules can directly interact with CQDs, converting them into oxygen and water. The presence of various functional groups on the surface of CQDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, enables the contribution of electrons to neutralize ROS, thus enhancing their scavenging ability. The % inhibition of H2O2 radicals was used to measure the antioxidant activity of the synthesized CQDs SB and SBP. [44]. The findings demonstrated a noteworthy inhibition in H2O2 free radicals in a way that was dose-dependent at 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 30 μg/mL, 40 μg/mL, and 50 μg/mL (Fig. 11 ). Antioxidant activity of different percentages of inhibition of oxidation such as, in SB 50 % of inhibition was observed at a concentration 10 μg/mL, 62 % of inhibition at 20 μg/mL, 63 % of inhibition at 30 μg/mL, 70 % of inhibition at 40 μg/mL and maximum anti-oxidant activity with 77 % of inhibition at 50 μg/mL (Fig. 11). Similarly, in SBP 50 % of inhibition was observed at a concentration 10 μg/mL, 59 % of inhibition at 20 μg/mL, 63 % of inhibition at 30 μg/mL, 68 % of inhibition at 40 μg/mL and maximum anti-oxidant activity with 79 % of inhibition at 50 μg/mL. CQDs SB and SBP also demonstrated noticeable radical scavenging activity. The findings showed that the potency of the CQDs was equivalent to that found in conventional standard ascorbic acid [45].

In the present study, we have reported an environment friendly green method for the synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots SB and SBP from sugarcane bagasse and pith. Several characterization studies were conducted on the CQDs to determine their appropriateness for various applications. CQDs from SB and SBP showing the quantum yield of 6.0 % and 6.4 % respectively. By using the H2O2 assay, CQDs SB and SBP both demonstrated notable free-radical scavenging activity, suggesting that they have antioxidant properties. Remarkable anti-inflammatory activity was obtained by egg albumin assay. Additionally, the lethality assay on brine shrimp verified that SB and SBP are not harmful in nature. The aforementioned investigations led to the biocompatibility using validation by H2O2 assays, egg albumin denaturation, and shrimp test. The above results proved to support this material as a possible active substitute for in vivo applications.
This work was supported by Kothari Sugars and Chemicals Ltd for financial assistance under the project no: LCK23PHY001/002/CH001/PB001.

K. Akash: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Albin John P Paul Winston: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Mohamed K.M.: Methodology, Data curation. P. Sagayaraj: Visualization, Resources, Conceptualization. J. Madhavan: Software, Formal analysis. S. Rajesh Kumar: Methodology, Conceptualization. S. Amala Jayanthi: Software, Investigation. J. Judith Vijaya: Software, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Loyola College for the infrastructural facilities and support offered in completing the present work and to Kothari Sugars and Chemicals Ltd for financial assistance.
